question: Does the NuScale ECCS design provide adequate core cooling during a loss-of-coolant accident?

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during all operational conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance by establishing steady-state parameters—such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures—across different power levels using thermal hydraulic modeling and verification. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how DHRS actuation valves are exercised and tested, primarily during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the procedures for verifying valve function and position, including a one-time in-situ test during initial startup.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It cites guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as relevant ASME code cases for materials, quality assurance, and inspection procedures for pressurized water reactors.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits specified in plant technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located within the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, ensuring safe cooling without external power during non-LOCA events. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, preventing fuel or pressure boundary limits from being exceeded, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear remaining the most likely degradation mechanism and corrosion risks assessed as low.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system. It specifies the types of steel and alloys used for various parts, their compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications, and highlights corrosion protection measures such as weld cladding with stainless steel and nickel-based alloys.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system elements, and the PZR, integrated with the RPV, helps maintain system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.
